suggestions.name: StreamCopier
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for copying data between streams efficiently. They represent different overloaded variants of the same core operation and already delegate to a StreamCopier class, making this extraction a natural fit that improves separation of concerns.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: copying data from input streams to output streams in large chunks. All methods are overloaded variants of `copyLarge` that handle different parameter combinations (with/without buffers, offsets, lengths) for both byte streams (InputStream/OutputStream) and character streams (Reader/Writer). They all delegate to a `StreamCopier` class, indicating they form a cohesive API facade.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "stream copying" domain. They represent different ways to perform the same fundamental operation: transferring data from a source to a destination stream efficiently. The methods handle both binary data (byte arrays) and text data (character arrays), but the core concept remains stream-to-stream data transfer.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates stream copying logic from other I/O utilities
- Code Reusability: Stream copying functionality can be reused independently across different contexts
- Complexity Reduction: Removes 8 related methods from the IOUtils "God Class", making it more focused
- Testability: Stream copying behavior can be tested in isolation with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate stream copying concerns. The methods form a cohesive unit with high internal cohesion (all perform stream copying) and low coupling (minimal dependencies). This extraction creates a specialized class focused on the single responsibility of efficient stream data transfer.

suggestions.name: StreamBufferFactory
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive factory for creating buffered I/O streams and readers/writers. They share the common responsibility of ensuring optimal I/O performance by wrapping unbuffered objects with their buffered counterparts while avoiding double-wrapping. Extracting them creates a focused factory class that centralizes buffering logic and reduces the complexity of the original IOUtils class.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: wrapping streams and readers/writers with their buffered counterparts when they aren't already buffered. Each method follows the same pattern - check if the input is already buffered, and if not, wrap it in the appropriate buffered implementation. They provide a safe, idempotent way to ensure buffering without double-wrapping.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "buffering" domain within I/O operations. They operate on the core Java I/O abstractions (InputStream, OutputStream, Reader, Writer) and transform them into their buffered equivalents. This represents a cohesive transformation concern - converting unbuffered I/O objects into buffered ones for performance optimization.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates buffering logic from other I/O utilities
- Code Reusability: Buffering logic can be reused across different I/O contexts
- Complexity Reduction: Removes 8 methods from the god class IOUtils, making it more focused
- Testability: Buffering behavior can be tested in isolation with focused unit tests
- Performance Optimization: Centralizes the logic for ensuring optimal I/O performance through buffering

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate cross-cutting concerns. The Factory pattern is also evident here, as these methods create buffered versions of I/O objects. The extracted class will have high cohesion (all methods ensure buffering) and low coupling (depends only on standard Java I/O classes).

suggestions.name: ResourceCloser
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused exclusively on safely closing and releasing system resources. They provide consistent resource cleanup strategies with different exception handling approaches (throwing vs. quiet suppression). Extracting them creates a dedicated component responsible for the critical task of resource lifecycle management.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: safely closing various types of resources (Closeable, URLConnection, Socket, etc.). They provide two main approaches - throwing exceptions on failure (close methods) or suppressing exceptions (closeQuietly methods). All methods handle null checks and provide safe resource cleanup.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "resource cleanup" domain. They handle the critical task of properly releasing system resources like streams, sockets, and connections. This is a fundamental concern in Java applications where improper resource management leads to memory leaks and resource exhaustion.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates resource cleanup logic from other IO operations
- Code Reusability: Resource cleanup can be used across different parts of an application
- Complexity Reduction: Removes resource management complexity from the main IOUtils class
- Testability: Resource cleanup behavior can be tested in isolation
- Error Handling Consistency: Centralizes different exception handling strategies for resource cleanup

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate resource lifecycle management concerns. The extracted class will have high cohesion (all methods deal with resource cleanup) and low coupling (minimal dependencies). It implements the Resource Acquisition Is Initialization (RAII) pattern support for Java.

suggestions.name: StreamSkipper
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused solely on skipping data in various input streams. Extracting them into a StreamSkipper class creates a specialized component with a single, clear responsibility: advancing position within input streams without reading data into memory.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: skipping over a specified number of bytes or characters in various input streams without reading the data into memory. All four methods perform the same core operation (skip) but handle different input types (InputStream, ReadableByteChannel, Reader) with optional buffer customization.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "stream navigation" domain, specifically the concept of advancing position within input streams. They represent a cohesive abstraction for bypassing data in sequential input sources without processing or storing that data. This is a specialized form of stream positioning operation.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates stream skipping logic from other I/O operations
- Code Reusability: Skip operations can be reused across different contexts without pulling in the entire IOUtils class
- Complexity Reduction: Removes specialized navigation logic from the general-purpose IOUtils class
- Testability: Stream skipping behavior can be tested in isolation with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate concerns. The methods form a cohesive unit with high internal cohesion (all perform skipping) and low coupling (minimal dependencies). This extraction creates a specialized component following the Single Responsibility Principle.

suggestions.name: StreamSkipper
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit focused on reliably skipping data from various input streams. They share the common responsibility of ensuring complete skipping operations across different stream types, warranting extraction into a specialized class that encapsulates this specific I/O operation.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, specific responsibility: skipping a specified number of bytes or characters from various input sources (InputStream, ReadableByteChannel, Reader) in a guaranteed manner. The "Fully" suffix indicates they ensure the exact amount is skipped, handling cases where the underlying skip() method might not skip the full amount in one call.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "stream skipping" domain. They provide a reliable skipping mechanism across different input stream types, abstracting away the complexity of ensuring complete skipping operations. They all delegate to StreamCopier, suggesting they're wrapper methods for a common skipping behavior.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Improves Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates guaranteed skipping logic from other I/O utilities
- Enhances Reusability: Skip functionality can be used independently without importing the entire IOUtils class
- Reduces Complexity: Removes specialized skipping methods from the god class, making it more focused
- Better Testability: Skip operations can be tested in isolation

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate a cohesive set of related operations. This creates a specialized utility focused solely on reliable stream skipping, adhering to the Single Responsibility Principle and reducing the surface area of the original utility class.

suggestions.name: StreamToCharArrayConverter
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive conversion unit that transforms various input sources (InputStreams and Readers) into character arrays. Extracting them into a dedicated converter class improves separation of concerns by isolating character array conversion logic from general I/O utilities, making the code more focused and reusable.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, focused responsibility: converting input streams and readers into character arrays. All four methods perform the same fundamental operation - reading data from various input sources (InputStream with different charset options, Reader) and transforming it into a char[] representation.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These methods belong to the "data conversion" domain, specifically stream-to-character-array conversion. They represent a cohesive abstraction for transforming streaming input data into in-memory character arrays, handling different input source types and character encoding scenarios.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates character array conversion logic from other I/O utilities
- Code Reusability: The converter can be used independently by classes that only need character array conversion
- Complexity Reduction: Removes conversion-specific logic from the broader IOUtils class
- Testability: Character array conversion logic can be tested in isolation with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate conversion concerns. The methods form a cohesive unit with high internal cohesion (all convert to char arrays) and low coupling (minimal dependencies). This aligns with the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a class dedicated solely to stream-to-character-array conversion.

suggestions.name: LineIteratorFactory
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive factory for creating LineIterator instances from various input sources. They share the single responsibility of LineIterator object creation and handle charset conversion complexity. Extracting them into a dedicated factory class improves separation of concerns and centralizes LineIterator creation logic.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, focused responsibility: creating LineIterator instances from different input sources. All three methods are factory methods that take various input types (InputStream with charset, InputStream with charset name, or Reader) and return LineIterator objects. They handle the complexity of wrapping input sources and charset conversion to produce a consistent LineIterator interface.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These methods belong to the "object creation" domain, specifically for LineIterator instances. They represent a cohesive factory pattern that abstracts the creation logic for LineIterators from different input sources. The methods form a family of overloaded factory methods that provide convenient ways to create the same type of object with different initialization parameters.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates LineIterator creation logic from general I/O utilities
- Code Reusability: The factory can be reused anywhere LineIterators need to be created
- Complexity Reduction: Removes object creation complexity from the main IOUtils class
- Testability: LineIterator creation logic can be tested independently
- Maintainability: Changes to LineIterator creation are centralized in one location

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Factory Method pattern and Extract Class refactoring. The methods represent a cohesive factory that should be separated from the general-purpose IOUtils class. This extraction supports the Single Responsibility Principle by giving LineIterator creation its own dedicated class with high cohesion and low coupling.

suggestions.name: DataWriter
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for writing various data types to output streams and writers. They share common concerns around data output, encoding handling, and writing strategies (chunked, line-based), making them ideal candidates for extraction into a specialized writing component that focuses solely on data output operations.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a common responsibility: writing data to output destinations (OutputStreams and Writers). They handle the conversion and transfer of various data types (byte arrays, char arrays, Strings, CharSequences, Collections) to different output targets, with support for character encoding and chunked writing strategies.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "data output/writing" domain. They represent a cohesive subsystem for writing different data formats to streams and writers, handling encoding concerns, and providing specialized writing strategies like chunked writing and line-based writing. The copyToOutputStream method also fits as it creates an output stream for data transfer.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Improves Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates all output writing logic from other I/O operations
- Enhances Reusability: Writing functionality can be used independently across different contexts
- Reduces Complexity: Removes a large cluster of related methods from the god class IOUtils
- Improves Testability: Writing operations can be tested in isolation with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate output writing concerns. This creates a specialized writer component that encapsulates various writing strategies and data type conversions, following the Single Responsibility Principle by focusing solely on data output operations.
